content: In the Bohr model of hydrogen atom, out of the following quantities, the principal quantum number is proportional to ( $\mathrm{R}, \mathrm{V}$ and E represent radius of the orbit, speed of electron and total energy of the electron respectively)
explanation: $$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \text { Radius }=\frac{\varepsilon_0 n^2 h^2}{\pi m_e z_e^2} \\ \therefore & R \propto n^2 \\ & \text { Velocity }=\frac{Z^2}{2 \varepsilon_0 n h} \\ & V \propto \frac{1}{n} \\ \therefore & \text { VR } \propto \frac{n^2}{n}=n \\ \therefore & \text { VR } \propto n \end{array} $$

content: According to Bohr's first postulate the kinetic energy $\frac{1}{2} m v^2$ of the electron in C.G.S. system is ( $\mathrm{m}=$ mass of electron, v is its velocity, r is the radius of the stationary orbit around the nucleus with charge Ze )
explanation:
Bohr’s first postulate (for circular orbit)

The centripetal force required to keep the electron in orbit is provided by the electrostatic Coulomb attraction between the electron $(-e)$ and the nucleus $(+Ze)$:

$ \text{Centripetal force} = \text{Coulomb force} $

$ \frac{m v^2}{r} = \frac{Z e^2}{r^2} $

Solve for $v^2$:

$ m v^2 = \frac{Z e^2}{r} $

Kinetic energy $T = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$:

$ T = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{Z e^2}{r} = \frac{Z e^2}{2r} $

✅ Therefore, the kinetic energy of the electron is:

$ \boxed{\frac{Z e^2}{2 r}} $

Correct Option:

Option C) $ \frac{Z e^2}{2r} $

content: In the spectrum of hydrogen atom the ratio of the longest wavelength in the Balmer series to the longest wavelength in the Lyman series is
explanation:
$$ \frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{~m}^2}\right) $$

For longest wavelength in Lyman series:

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{n}=1, \mathrm{~m}=2 \\ & \frac{1}{\lambda_{\mathrm{L}}}=\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i) \end{aligned} $$

For longest wavelength in Balmer series:

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{n}=2, \mathrm{~m}=3 \\ & \frac{1}{\lambda_{\mathrm{B}}}=\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right) \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{aligned} $$

Dividing equation (i) by equation (ii),

$$ \frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{B}}}{\lambda_{\mathrm{L}}}=\frac{R\left(1-\frac{1}{4}\right)}{R\left(\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{9}\right)}=\frac{\frac{3}{4}}{\frac{5}{36}}==\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{36}{5}=\frac{27}{5} $$

content: The series limit for the frequency of Balmer series is ' $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{B}}$ ', then the series limit frequency of Paschen series ' $v_p$ ' is
explanation:
The series limit frequency of Balmer series is observed when $\mathrm{n}=2$ and $\mathrm{n}=\infty$

$$ \therefore \quad \mathrm{h} v_{\mathrm{B}}=\mathrm{E}_0\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{\infty^2}\right]=\frac{\mathrm{E}_0}{4} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

For Paschen series, $\mathrm{n}=3$ and $\mathrm{n}=\infty$

$$ h v_p=E_0\left[\frac{1}{9}\right] \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)$$

Dividing (i) by (ii)

$$ \begin{aligned} & 9 v_{\mathrm{P}}=4 v_{\mathrm{B}} \\ & v_{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{4}{9} v_{\mathrm{B}} \end{aligned} $$

content: Radioactive materials A and B have decay constants ' $9 \lambda$ ' and ' $\lambda$ ' respectively. Initially they have same number of nuclei. The ratio of number of nuclei of material ' $A$ ' to that of ' $B$ ' will be $\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}}\right)$ after time ' $t$ '. So ' $t$ ' is equal to
explanation:
Let the initial number of nuclei in both A and B be $N_0$.

For radioactive decay, the number of nuclei at time $t$ is:

$$ N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t} $$

For material A (with decay constant $9\lambda$):

$$ N_A = N_0 e^{-9\lambda t} $$

For material B (with decay constant $\lambda$):

$$ N_B = N_0 e^{-\lambda t} $$

Given:

$$ \frac{N_A}{N_B} = \frac{1}{e} $$

Substitute the expressions:

$ \frac{N_0 e^{-9\lambda t}}{N_0 e^{-\lambda t}} = \frac{1}{e} $

$ e^{-9\lambda t + \lambda t} = \frac{1}{e} $

$ e^{-8\lambda t} = e^{-1} $

Comparing the powers:

$ -8\lambda t = -1 $

$ 8\lambda t = 1 $

$ t = \frac{1}{8\lambda} $

Correct option:

$ \boxed{\frac{1}{8\lambda}} $

So, the answer is Option C.

content: If $R_1$ and $R_2$ are the radii of the atomic nuclei of mass numbers 27 and 125 respectively, then the ratio $R_2: R_1$ is
explanation:
The radius of a nucleus is given by the formula:

$$ R = R_0 A^{1/3} $$

where $R_0$ is a constant and $A$ is the mass number.

Given:

  • $R_1$: radius for mass number $A_1 = 27$

  • $R_2$: radius for mass number $A_2 = 125$

The ratio is:

$ \frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{R_0 A_2^{1/3}}{R_0 A_1^{1/3}} = \left( \frac{A_2}{A_1} \right)^{1/3} $

Substitute the values:

$ \frac{R_2}{R_1} = \left( \frac{125}{27} \right)^{1/3} $

Now, calculate:

  • $125 = 5^3$

  • $27 = 3^3$

So,

$ \left( \frac{125}{27} \right)^{1/3} = \frac{5}{3} $

Correct Answer:

Option C

$\boxed{\frac{5}{3}}$

content: The angular momentum of electron in hydrogen atom in first orbit is ' $L$ '. The change in angular momentum if electron is in second orbit of hydrogen atom is

If an electron in hydrogen atom jumps from $3^{\text {rd }}$ orbit to $2^{\text {nd }}$ orbit it emits a photon of wavelength ' $\lambda$ '. When it emits a photon from $4^{\text {th }}$ orbit to $3^{\text {rd }}$ orbit then the corresponding wavelength of emitted photon will be
